title: Automating AAPOR Survey Response Rate Calculations
summary: Built an R pipeline that replaces hours of manual AAPOR response-rate calculation with a single script, producing clean, standardized rate tables across multiple surveys in seconds.

sources.title: AAPOR Standard Definitions (outcome rate formulas)
sources.publisher: American Association for Public Opinion Research

sources.title: Best Research Presentation Award — Ted Winnowski Business Conference 2025
SUNY International Business Analytics Conference 2025 Proceedings

sections.overview: At Siena Research Institute, calculating standard AAPOR outcome rates for each completed survey meant manually reading raw survey response files and hand-filling AAPOR's official Excel template — a process that took hours per survey and had to be repeated for every new dataset.
sections.problem: AAPOR's response-rate formulas (response, cooperation, refusal, and contact rate) are precisely defined but tedious and error-prone to apply by hand at scale, especially across many surveys with slightly different raw data formats.
sections.approach: I built an R script that ingests raw survey response files directly, replicates AAPOR's official template logic programmatically, and outputs a single clean, standardized table of rates per survey — turning an hours-long manual process into a seconds-long automated one.
methodology: Studied AAPOR's Standard Definitions document to translate its official Excel-template formulas for response, cooperation, refusal, and contact rate into equivalent R logic.
Built an R pipeline to read raw survey outcome files, clean and categorize dispositions according to AAPOR's coding rules, and apply the rate formulas automatically.
Designed the output as a single consolidated table so that results for multiple surveys could be reviewed side by side instead of one Excel file per survey.
Validated the automated output against manually-calculated AAPOR template results to confirm the formulas were replicated correctly.

results.summary: The tool reduced a manual, per-survey process taking hours to a single run producing standardized rates across multiple surveys in seconds, with results matching the manual AAPOR template calculations.
points: Automated calculation of all four AAPOR outcome rates: response rate, cooperation rate, refusal rate, and contact rate.
Replaced a one-survey-at-a-time manual workflow with a single script capable of processing multiple survey files into one consolidated table.

lessons: Codifying an official methodology (AAPOR's) into code forces you to understand every edge case in the formulas far more precisely than filling out a template by hand ever does.

Won Best Research Presentation at Siena's Ted Winnowski Business Conference (2025) for this work, and it was separately presented by a co-author at the SUNY International Business Analytics Conference, Fredonia (2025).

title: Knowledge Graph vs. Vector RAG: A Comparative Study
summary: Research comparing vector-based and knowledge-graph-based retrieval-augmented generation, presented at the NY UPSTAT Conference 2026.

sections.overview: This research examines whether representing knowledge explicitly as a graph — rather than retrieving text purely by semantic similarity — produces better-reasoned, more explainable answers in a retrieval-augmented generation system, presented at the NY UPSTAT Conference 2026.
sections.problem: Standard vector RAG retrieves text by similarity, but similarity isn't the same as connection: it struggles to trace multi-step cause-and-effect chains and offers no visibility into why a given answer was produced. The research question was how a graph-based retrieval approach compares against a standard vector-retrieval baseline on exactly those points.
sections.approach: The study compared a vector-retrieval baseline against a graph-based pipeline built to support multi-hop reasoning and auditability, using a COVID-19 knowledge graph as the test domain, evaluating both on relationship-heavy, multi-hop questions that plain similarity search tends to struggle with.
methodology: Built a knowledge graph from source text as (Subject, Predicate, Object) triples with typed relationships (e.g. CAUSES, REQUIRES).
Implemented a graph-based retrieval pipeline using hybrid entity matching, 1-hop fact retrieval, and explicit 2-hop path traversal for multi-step reasoning.
Compared this against a standard vector-similarity retrieval baseline on the same set of questions, with particular attention to multi-hop 'how/why' questions.
Presented the comparative findings at the NY UPSTAT Conference 2026.

results.summary: The graph-based approach produced answers grounded in explicit, inspectable evidence chains, directly addressing the multi-hop reasoning and explainability limitations identified in the vector-only baseline.
points: Graph traversal surfaced multi-step reasoning chains (e.g., cause → effect → downstream consequence) that similarity search alone did not reliably retrieve.
Every graph-based answer could be traced back to specific facts and relationship paths, in contrast to the vector baseline's similarity-score-only justification.

lessons: Vector and graph retrieval aren't really in competition — the strongest design uses vector similarity for narrow tasks (like entity matching) and graph structure for the actual reasoning.
Presenting comparative, not just descriptive, research forces much sharper thinking about what specifically each method is and isn't good at.
